col-gha.title: Colombia–Ghana: the Black Stars want to reignite the memory
col-gha.angle: Colombia brings pace and sharp attacking talent. Ghana brings a World Cup memory too heavy to be treated as a simple outsider story.
body: Colombia–Ghana is a match of rhythm, talent and memory. Colombia arrive with Luis Díaz, intensity, acceleration and the ability to turn one action into a storm.
But Ghana are not just another World Cup opponent. The Black Stars carry 2006, 2010, 2014, Abedi Pelé’s legacy and Asamoah Gyan’s generation — a history that still weighs heavily in knockout nights.
Today, Mohammed Kudus embodies a new kind of energy: modern, explosive, capable of breaking lines and giving Ghana the unpredictability that can terrify a favourite.
Colombia will have their weapons. Ghana will have their memory. And sometimes, in a World Cup, memory weighs as much as a game plan.

porcro-spain.title: Portugal–Spain: an Iberian derby with history on the line
porcro-spain.angle: Neighbours, giants and future 2030 World Cup co-hosts with Morocco: this Round of 16 has the scent of a true derby.
body: Portugal–Spain is never neutral. It is a border, a memory, a rivalry between neighbours who know each other too well to be naive.
Portugal arrive after beating Croatia 2–1. Cristiano Ronaldo remains the symbol, the face that turns every Portuguese night into an event.
Spain have just controlled Austria 3–0. La Roja want the ball, the rhythm and the nerves. But against Portugal, control is never enough for long.
The history adds spice: 11v11 lists 41 meetings, with Spain on 18 wins, 16 draws and Portugal on 7 wins.
And 2030 sits in the background: Spain, Portugal and Morocco will host the next World Cup together. Before sharing the world stage, these neighbours fight to stay alive in this one.
